Title: Juru-Sungai Dua traffic project remains on track, hits 28.75 pct progress
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Juru-Sungai Dua Traffic Dispersal Project (PTJSD) continues to record positive progress, with Package 1 of the project achieving 28.75 per cent overall completion as of July 12 and remaining on schedule.
PLUS Malaysia Berhad (PLUS) said major milestones achieved include the completion of preliminary works, while utility relocation works have reached 70 per cent and geotechnical works have recorded 68 per cent progress.
"Package 1 involves the upgrading of the East-West Roundabout, improvements to the traffic light system at the roundabout to ease traffic flow, and the construction of a new elevated slip road at Jalan Tun Hussein Onn," it said in a Facebook post.
PLUS said that upon completion in October 2027, PTJSD would help improve traffic flow, reduce congestion, enhance safety and provide a more comfortable travel experience for highway users and local residents.
The 17.3-kilometre project, spanning three main districts — South, Central and North Seberang Perai — is expected to benefit 200,000 daily users of the route.
The PTJSD, estimated to cost RM3 billion, is expected to ease congestion, with 30 per cent of traffic projected to shift to the new direct Juru-Sungai Dua route. This will reduce the current travel time from one hour to just 20 minutes during peak hours.
The project is being implemented in collaboration with the Ministry of Works and the Malaysian Highways Authority to address congestion along the Juru-Sungai Dua corridor, which serves as the main route connecting Penang with states in the northern peninsula.
—Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC seeks two Bangladeshi witnesses in corruption trial
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) is seeking two Bangladeshi men to testify as prosecution witnesses in an ongoing corruption trial at the Kuala Lumpur Sessions Court.
The case, involving charges under Section 16(a)(B) of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2009, is scheduled for hearings this month, as well as in August and September.
MACC said efforts to locate the first witness, Fardin Mahi, at his last known address at Menara City One, Jalan Munshi Abdullah, Kuala Lumpur, were unsuccessful.
Another prosecution witness, Md Sumon Hossain, also could not be traced at his last known address at Residensi Pandanmas, Kampung Pandan, Kuala Lumpur.
Members of the public with information on the whereabouts of either man are urged to contact investigating officer Ps Mohd Hafifi Rahmat at 018-6632034, the MACC Kuala Lumpur office at 03-9206 5000, or email hafifi.rahmat@sprm.gov.my.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Why has the Iran-US ceasefire memorandum frayed?
Article:
RENEWED US-Iranian hostilities are eroding an interim agreement signed in June, reflecting disagreements over the Strait of Hormuz and other issues and underlining difficulties facing efforts to secure lasting peace.
The 14-point Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) declared an end to the war and the Strait of Hormuz open for commercial vessels.
But its wording was vague on key points, analysts say, while leaving tough issues - notably the fate of Iran's nuclear programme - to a second phase of negotiations.
WHAT HAVE THE PARTIES SAID RECENTLY ABOUT THE DEAL?
US President Donald Trump declared the initial ceasefire agreement "over" last week, saying Iranian officials were not honouring deals they negotiated.
On Monday, he said the United States would probably take over the Strait of Hormuz.
Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei accused Washington on Monday of driving the MoU "into crisis", saying Washington has consistently violated its commitments.
Mediator Pakistan has urged all sides to uphold their commitments.
WHAT'S AT ISSUE IN THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ?
After the start of the war, which began with US-Israeli strikes on Iran on Feb 28, Teheran effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas had been shipped.
Article 5 of the MoU says that the traffic of commercial vessels would immediately start and that Iran "will make arrangements using its best efforts for the safe passage of commercial vessels with no charge for 60 days only, from the Persian Gulf to the Sea of Oman and vice versa."
Teheran interprets this as US recognition of Iran's right to manage the whole waterway, albeit without charging fees or tolls for two months.
The United States - and Gulf states - reject that interpretation, regarding the language as meaning only that Iran should facilitate safe passage for vessels and not impose restrictions backed up by force.
The US has said the strait will remain toll-free. Over the last week, Iran has fired on vessels it says have tried to sail through the waterway on an unapproved route, and declared it closed again.
The US Navy's Joint Maritime Information Center said on Sunday a southern route through the strait remains available and has been expanded to accommodate two-way traffic.
WHAT IS HAPPENING TO US OIL WAIVERS FOR IRANIAN OIL SALES?
Article 10 says Washington will issue waivers allowing for the export of Iranian crude oil, petroleum products, and all associated services, including banking transactions, insurance and transportation.
This was a big win for Iran, its economy suffocated for years by sanctions.
But on July 7, the US revoked a license allowing Iran to sell oil, warning that Iranian actions in the Strait of Hormuz were "wholly unacceptable" and would be met with consequences.
Teheran condemned this as a breach of the MoU.
[[nid:1488500]]
WHAT IS THE STATUS OF IRAN'S FROZEN ASSETS?
Article 11 says the US "undertakes to make fully available for use the frozen or restricted funds and assets" of Iran, and that Teheran and Washington would agree on procedures related to the release of these funds during the negotiation.
The assets include US$6 billion held in Qatari accounts. Qatar said on June 30 that it had not transferred these funds to Teheran.
On June 22, US Vice President JD Vance said the US and Qatar would have control over the funds when they are unfrozen, and that the money could be spent on US corn, soy and wheat.
In response, Ali Bahreini, Iran's ambassador to the United ​Nations in Geneva, said Iran alone would decide how to use unfrozen assets.
HOW DOES LEBANON FIT IN?
Iran's parliament speaker and chief negotiator Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf on July 8 cited Israeli attacks in Lebanon as a violation of the MoU.
Lebanon was pulled into the conflict when Iran-backed Hizbollah opened fire at Israel on March 2, leading to an Israeli offensive and invasion of the south.
Iran had said Israel must cease fire in Lebanon as part of the deal.
WHERE DOES THIS LEAVE WIDER NEGOTIATIONS?
The MoU says Washington and Teheran commit to negotiating a final deal in a maximum of 60 days, extendable by mutual consent.
But with control over the Strait of Hormuz contested, the sides have not declared any date for further talks.
It took years for Iran to negotiate its 2015 nuclear agreement with world powers. Trump withdrew the US from that deal during his first term.
"The MoU is in crisis and you now need a secondary deal to restore it, if it is to be a basis for restoring calm," said Mohanad Hage Ali of the Carnegie Middle East Center think tank.
"The vagueness reflected the difficulty of the issues and the fragility of the agreement."
* The article is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Negri Sembilan polls: PN to unveil candidates on Thursday
Article:
KOTA BARU: Perikatan Nasional will announce its Negri Sembilan election candidates on Thursday, a day after Barisan Nasional unveils its line-up.
PN information chief Tan Sri Annuar Musa said the timing would provide a clearer picture of the coalition's electoral strategy ahead of nomination day.
"Tomorrow night, BN will announce its line-up. A day later, on Thursday, PN will announce its list of candidates.
"From there, all of you will understand why the nomination process has unfolded the way it has," he told reporters here today.
Separately, Annuar confirmed that Pas would meet tonight at its headquarters to finalise its contested seats. The meeting will be chaired by party president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang.
"Pas has already identified the seats it intends to contest. God willing, all our candidates will emerge victorious this time," he said.
Annuar, however, declined to disclose the specific constituencies, saying details would only be revealed during Thursday's official announcement.
Pas' meeting comes in the wake of Hadi's announcement that the Islamist party was in
discussions with Umno
over cooperation in Negri Sembilan.
However, Umno president Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi later dismissed the claim, saying Umno had
no agreement
with Pas on seats in Negri Sembilan.
The 16th Negri Sembilan state election is scheduled for Aug 1, with nomination day set for July 18.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Forum seeks stronger animal welfare framework, regional cooperation
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Malaysia is stepping up efforts to strengthen its animal welfare framework through greater regional cooperation, policy development and the adoption of science-based practices at the Animal Welfare Forum 2026 held here.
The two-day forum, which began today, brings together government agencies, academic institutions and international organisations to discuss emerging challenges and strategies to improve animal welfare standards in Malaysia and the Asia-Pacific region.
The Department of Veterinary Services (DVS) said the forum was organised by its Veterinary Regulatory Division in strategic collaboration with A World of Good Initiative Inc.
"Among the key topics discussed are effective animal welfare governance, the implementation of animal welfare strategies in the Asia-Pacific region, the One Welfare concept, community well-being, livestock welfare challenges, poultry welfare and aquaculture welfare," it said in a statement today.
DVS said the forum also features experts from Australia, the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH), the World Federation for Animals (WFA), as well as local and international higher education institutions.
The forum aligns with the Malaysian Animal Welfare Strategic Plan 2021-2030, which aims to enhance governance, compliance and implementation of animal welfare practices through a strategic approach based on science, innovation and collaboration.
Veterinary Services director-general Datuk Dr Mohd Noor Hisham Mohd Haron said animal welfare had evolved beyond being a secondary concern, becoming an important element in building a modern society founded on kindness, sustainability and shared responsibility.
He said effective animal welfare practices were closely linked to public health, food safety and security, environmental sustainability and community well-being, in line with the internationally recognised One Health and One Welfare approaches.
"In line with WOAH standards, good animal welfare practices not only improve animal welfare but also contribute to a more sustainable, productive and resilient food production system, especially as Malaysia continues to face high demand for animal-based protein sources," he said.
Meanwhile, A World of Good Initiative Inc chief executive officer Dr Michelle Sinclair said strengthening the implementation of the Animal Welfare Act 2015 required policymakers and stakeholders to remain updated on global developments and best practices.
She said the involvement of international experts would also support the development of science-based and evidence-based policies.
–Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Jailed 'Geng Upik' leader charged with RM800,000 money laundering
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: A convicted member of the organised crime group 'Geng Upik' was charged in the Sessions Court here today with nine counts of money laundering involving more than RM800,000.
The accused, Datuk Maslan Sani, 42, pleaded not guilty before Judge Zaini Fishir @ Fisal.
Maslan was charged with receiving proceeds of unlawful activities amounting to RM5,500, RM20,000, RM175,090, RM33,990, RM4,170, RM29,900, RM284,400, RM139,700 and RM129,200 through deposits into two of his bank accounts.
The offences were allegedly committed between January and December 2016 at several CIMB Bank branches in Kota Kinabalu, Tawau and Lahad Datu.
He was charged under Section 4(1)(b) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001, punishable under Section 4(1) of the same Act.
If convicted, he faces up to 15 years' imprisonment and a fine of not less than five times the amount or value of the proceeds of the unlawful activity or the instrumentalities of the offence at the time it was committed, or RM5 million, whichever is higher.
The court fixed Aug 11 for pre-trial case management.
No bail application was made as Maslan is currently serving a five-year prison sentence.
For the record,
the High Court on June 5, 2025
sentenced Maslan to five years' imprisonment after he pleaded guilty to being a member of the organised crime group between 2015 and 2023.
According to court records, Maslan was the leader of the group.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Mohd Ashrof Adrin Kamarul appeared for the prosecution, while Maslan was represented by lawyers Abdul Fikry Jaafar Abdullah and Mohd Azhier Farhan Arisin.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Melaka govt allocates RM200,000 to empower cooperatives this year, says exco
Article:
MELAKA: The Melaka government has allocated RM200,000 this year to empower multi-cluster cooperatives under the E-PERKASA initiative.
State Entrepreneur Development, Cooperatives and Consumer Affairs Committee chairman Allex Seah Shoo Chin said grants of up to RM20,000 are available for multi-cluster cooperatives, while newly established cooperatives can receive up to RM50,000.
"The scope of assistance includes grants of up to RM10,000 to finance business development costs, such as the purchase of equipment, machinery and new business fittings, including computerised automation systems.
"These include shelving, automatic and semi-automatic machines, business signboards, security systems and other related equipment.
"The assistance also covers the purchase of IT systems, as well as the development of innovation and business marketing platforms," he said during the Melaka State Assembly sitting here today.
He was responding to a question from Datuk Zahari Abd Kalil (BN-Durian Tunggal), who asked whether the state government planned to establish a Melaka Cooperative Empowerment Fund to provide working capital injections for cooperatives with a proven track record but facing constraints in expanding their businesses.
Seah said the state government also provides grants of up to RM10,000 to finance working capital for business operations or additional capital to expand business activities.
He added that grants of up to RM5,000 are also available for new cooperatives registered in 2026 to finance the purchase of equipment, machinery and business fittings, including working capital for business operations.
—Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: A-G–public prosecutor split Bill deferred pending Rulers' briefing
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The constitutional amendment to separate the roles of the Attorney-General and public prosecutor will be deferred to the next Dewan Rakyat sitting pending a briefing with the Conference of Rulers.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the briefing would include an additional amendment aimed at strengthening Parliament's role in the appointment process.
She said the move respected the institution of the Constitutional Monarchy and ensured the reform process was carried out in an orderly and prudent manner.
"The government intends to proceed with the second and third readings of the Constitutional (Amendment) (No.2) Bill 2026, including debate and decision-making, at the next Dewan Rakyat sitting after the Conference of Rulers has been briefed on the proposed amendment," she told the Dewan Rakyat.
Azalina was presenting the report of the
Special Select Committee
on the Bill.
The Bill was referred to the committee in March after being tabled in February, following calls from opposition lawmakers for improvements to its implementation.
She said the government was now proposing a further amendment to Clause 18 of Article 145A to give Parliament a stronger role in the appointment process.
Under the proposed mechanism, the candidate's name would first be submitted to the Dewan Rakyat Speaker for tabling in the House, where it would be scrutinised by a Select Committee before the House makes a recommendation to the Judicial and Legal Service Commission.
The commission would then advise the King based solely on the candidate recommended by the Dewan Rakyat.
Azalina said the committee had outlined seven key recommendations, including appointing the public prosecutor on the advice of the Judicial and Legal Service Commission without involvement from the prime minister or cabinet, a fixed seven-year term with no renewal, annual reports to both Houses of Parliament, and a dedicated code of ethics for the office.
She said civil society groups had described the reform as "not a victory for any political party, but a victory for the rule of law".
"This reform is not about political interests, but about strengthening the rule of law, empowering national institutions and increasing public confidence in the justice system," she said.
Currently, the Attorney-General serves as both the government's chief legal adviser and the final authority on criminal prosecutions.
The proposed reform seeks to separate the two roles to strengthen checks and balances and address concerns over potential conflicts of interest.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia pushes for stronger Asean role in refugee crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is seeking stronger burden-sharing among Asean member countries to address the regional refugee crisis, alongside closer cooperation with international migration and humanitarian organisations.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said regional efforts should also focus on achieving a political solution that would allow Rohingya refugees to return home voluntarily, safely and with dignity.
He said Malaysia would continue strengthening cooperation with Asean and the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) through a more strategic and comprehensive approach.
"Measures that could be considered include strengthening responsibility-sharing mechanisms among Asean member states, expanding cooperation with international organisations involved in migration and humanitarian affairs, and encouraging a political solution for the voluntary, safe and dignified return of Rohingya refugees," he said.
Zahid said this in a parliamentary written reply to Datuk Seri Jalaluddin Alias (BN-Jelebu), who asked about government measures to manage refugees registered with UNHCR.
He said the government had identified three approaches to managing UNHCR cardholders in Malaysia – voluntary repatriation, resettlement in third countries and deportation of those who violate Malaysian laws.
Integrated operations involving security and enforcement agencies, as well as local authorities, are also being carried out nationwide to address non-compliance among foreign migrants.
Zahid said Malaysia was developing its own mechanism to register and monitor refugees and asylum seekers through the Refugee Registration Document (DPP) programme before they are returned to their countries of origin or resettled in third countries.
The mechanism would also allow eligible refugees access to healthcare, education and employment while safeguarding national security and sovereignty.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foodie Media posts RM4.15mil earnings in Q3
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Foodie Media Bhd recorded a net profit of RM4.15 million in the third quarter ended May 31, 2026 (Q3 FY26), reflecting lower one-off initial public offering listing expenses.
Revenue stood at RM13.9 million, driven by its content and creator pillars, which include sponsored content and key opinion leader (KOL) marketing activities.
There are no comparative figures for the same quarter last year as this is the fourth interim financial report prepared for the third quarter ended 31 May 2026.
For the nine-month period, Foodie Media recorded revenue of RM39.7 million and a net profit of RM11 million.
Foodie Media had RM49.5 million in cash and short-term deposits, with total borrowings of RM1.1 million comprising hire purchase and lease liabilities.
The company was in a net cash position of about RM48.4 million.
In a statement, Foodie Media said its owned portfolio of 45 brands with a combined following of more than 50 million allows it to generate revenue across multiple streams, reducing reliance on any single business segment.
It expects demand for integrated marketing solutions to continue growing as advertisers increasingly seek partners that can deliver content production, influencer marketing, campaign execution and consumer engagement through a single platform.
Foodie Media said it would continue expanding into new content verticals, industry segments and client categories while leveraging technology and artificial intelligence to improve productivity and campaign performance.
Chief executive officer Nicholas Lim Pinn Yang said the group's nine-month performance demonstrated the strength of its integrated platform and diversified business model.
""Backed by a strong balance sheet and supported by favourable industry trends, we remain focused on scaling the audience we own, investing in technology and talent and deepening how we monetise that audience across the 4Cs -Content, Creator, Commerce and Community - as we work towards becoming the attention infrastructure of Southeast Asia," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Witness Protection Programme to be strengthened with wider access, stronger safeguards
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government is seeking to expand access to the Witness Protection Programme, improve participants' welfare, and extend protections to former participants even after they leave the scheme.
Presenting the Witness Protection (Amendment) Bill for its second reading in the Dewan Rakyat, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the proposed amendments were built on four main objectives.
They are to expand access to the programme, strengthen its governance and administration, improve participants' welfare and well-being, and extend protection to former participants after they leave the programme.
Azalina said participation in the programme had more than doubled since 2020, rising from 34 to 78 in 2025, reflecting growing demand for the protection it offers across a range of serious criminal cases, including murder, organised crime, drug trafficking, kidnapping, corruption and sexual offences.
Among the proposed amendments, the Bill would allow a parent or guardian to apply for entry into the programme on behalf of a witness who lacks legal capacity, and include psychological assistance, including counselling, among the forms of assistance that may be provided to participants.
The Bill also proposes giving the programme's director-general the power to issue standing orders for its administration and implementation.
She said the amendments would not alter the fundamental policy of the existing Act but would strengthen its legislative framework based on 15 years of implementation experience and current operational needs.
Azalina said the amendments reflected the government's commitment to protecting those with the courage to come forward with the truth.
"Every individual who comes forward to give testimony or cooperate with the authorities must receive appropriate protection, without fear, threats or intimidation," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Expert witness in Zara inquest is not a registered child clinical psychologist
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The Coroner's Court was told that the 76th witness in the Zara Qairina Mahathir inquest - Dr Noor Aishah Rosli - was not a registered child clinical psychologist under the  Malaysian Allied Health Professions Council (MAHPC).
Noor Aishah, however, told the court she had two qualifications as registered counsellor and child clinical psychologist.
Conducting officer Nahra Dollah said they have no evidence that the witness is a registered child psychologist under the Allied Health Professions Act 2016.
Nahra:
Doctor, you are not a child clinical psychologist registered under that Act?
Noor Aishah:
I am not registered under that Act, but I rely on my registration with the Counsellors Board. I have expertise in this field through my academic background and a clinical psychology training licence from the United States.
Nahra:
Do you agree with me that the only evidence we have is that you are a registered counsellor and not a registered child clinical psychologist?
Doctor:
Yes.
The 51-year-old witness added that under the Counsellors Board, its members are allowed to practice and their statements and assessments involving children and adolescents can be used in court.
Noor Aishah also told the court she had used the association membership number instead of the designated number meant for registered clinical child psychologist under the Act.
Nahra:
Doctor, do you know that it is an offence? Doctor had used the designation as child clinical psychologist without registering under the said Act?
Lawyer Shahlan Jufri, who is representing the late Zara Qairina Mahathir's mother, objected as he believed the witness could not answer on the issue of the said law.
Nahra, however, said that it is a basic matter of credibility in court.
The inquest will resume at 2.30pm.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: iCaur V23: Dares to be different
Article:
In a market where most electric SUVs chase sleek aerodynamics and futuristic styling, the icaur V23 goes in the opposite direction.
It is unapologetically boxy, rugged and retro, borrowing cues from iconic off-roaders while remaining unmistakably modern.
Launched in Malaysia at RM119,800 for the rear-wheel-drive variant and RM132,800 for the all-wheel-drive iWD, the V23 occupies an increasingly competitive price band.
Yet rather than competing on outright performance or range, it sells a lifestyle proposition - one that blends urban practicality with adventure-inspired styling.
A Design With Genuine Character
[[nid:1488462]]
The V23's biggest strength is undoubtedly its design.
Circular LED headlamps, a near-vertical windscreen, chunky wheel arches and a side-opening tailgate give it an identity few EVs can match.
It evokes hints of the classic Land Rover Defender, Toyota Land Cruiser and Mercedes-Benz G-Class without directly copying any of them.
Unlike many crossover EVs that disappear into traffic, the V23 commands attention. Buyers looking for individuality will find few alternatives at this price point.
Built For More Than City Streets
Despite its retro appearance, the V23 is more than a styling exercise.
It boasts up to 210mm of ground clearance, a 600mm wading depth, generous approach and departure angles, and six driving modes on the iWD variant.
This is one of the few electric SUVs in its class that can venture confidently onto gravel roads, campsites and light off-road trails.
It is not intended to rival hardcore 4x4s, but it offers capability beyond what most urban EV buyers are likely to need.
The dual-motor iWD produces 211PS and 292Nm, reaching 100kph in 7.5 seconds, while the entry-level rear-wheel-drive model develops 136PS and completes the sprint in 11 seconds.
Those figures are respectable rather than thrilling.
Acceleration feels smooth and predictable instead of explosive.
The V23 is tuned for relaxed driving, with suspension that favours comfort over outright sportiness. The upright driving position and excellent visibility make it particularly easy to place on narrow Malaysian roads.
Cabin With The Right Balance
Inside, the V23 adopts a refreshingly practical approach.
A 15.4-inch touchscreen dominates the dashboard, but unlike many Chinese EVs, key functions are complemented by physical switches and rotary controls.
This makes adjusting the air-conditioning or drive settings far less distracting while driving.
[[nid:1488461]]
The cabin also offers powered and ventilated front seats, wireless Apple CarPlay and Android Auto, NFC key access and thoughtful storage solutions.
Passenger space benefits from the long wheelbase, although the boot is relatively modest with the rear seats in place.
Where Compromises Emerge
The V23 is not without shortcomings.
Its claimed 360km (2WD) and 430km (iWD) NEDC range translates into considerably lower real-world driving distances, especially on highways.
Charging speeds of 85kW and 104kW DC are competitive but no longer class-leading as newer rivals push beyond 150kW.
The infotainment interface, while feature-rich, can also feel less polished than systems offered by some established EV competitors.
Excellent Value
[[nid:1488460]]
What ultimately makes the V23 compelling is not any single specification but the overall package.
For around RM130,000, buyers get distinctive styling, respectable electric performance, genuine light off-road ability, generous equipment and comprehensive warranty coverage.
Early buyers also benefited from complimentary charging equipment and extended battery protection.
Verdict
The iCAUR V23 succeeds because it refuses to follow the conventional EV formula.
Instead of chasing headline acceleration figures or maximum driving range, it delivers personality, practicality and versatility in equal measure.
It feels like a lifestyle vehicle rather than merely another electric SUV.
Its charging speeds and range no longer set the benchmark, but its design, everyday usability and strong value proposition more than compensate.
For buyers seeking an EV that stands apart from the growing sea of anonymous crossovers, the icaur V23 is arguably one of the most distinctive and likeable choices currently on sale in Malaysia.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thai bandmates recount chaos of deadly Bangkok bar fire [WATCH]
Article:
BANGKOK: First the band saw smoke. Then the lights went out and an explosive fire ripped through the Bangkok bar and restaurant, spraying flames from the entrance into the street.
The inferno left at least 28 people dead, including two members of the cover band on Sunday night's bill.
At least two other bandmates were injured, among around 70 people who suffered from burns, smoke inhalation and other injuries, authorities said.
Two members of the group Tosakan recounted on Monday the chaos of the blaze that raged through the Rong Beer Na Lat Phrao bar and restaurant, claiming the lives of their keyboardist, Kwang, and a singer, Breeze.
"The sight of my girlfriend's body being carried out, the sight of my friend who was burned in the fire and everything that happened are still etched in my mind," said singer and band leader Athipat "Ice" Wijarn.
Some members of his 11-person group, which plays Thai and international cover songs, were on stage when the fire broke out around 11:00 pm (1600 GMT) Sunday, Athipat said in a talk show interview.
He sustained a head injury and his drummer was in critical condition.
"Our keyboard player spotted the smoke first and shouted at me to run," Athipat later recalled, speaking to reporters.
"When I realised there was a fire, I could smell smoke... I shouted for my girlfriend (Breeze), but then the power went out," he added.
The pitch-black venue was also clouded in thick smoke, and the singer could not find Breeze, despite shouting her name, he said.
"Just as I got outside, the explosion suddenly erupted," Athipat said, showing his injuries.
[[nid:1488485]]
'I JUST RAN'
He tried to revive Breeze by giving her mouth-to-mouth resuscitation when her body was brought outside, but it was too late.
"I felt terrible," he said, full of regret that he did not tell patrons to evacuate at the first sign of fire.
The venue had power outages before due to electrical problems, so he had assumed it was just another power glitch.
"I still feel I reacted too slowly. I should have shouted to warn everyone, but everything happened so fast and there was so much chaos that I didn't have time."
Bassist Anan "Tor" Prasert, 37, said he received minor burns but escaped the blaze relatively quickly -- with his bass guitar still slung over his shoulder.
"At first I thought it was just the stage smoke, you know?" Anan told reporters. "Then smoke started coming out from the ceiling.
He told a guitarist next to him to run, Anan said.
"At that point, I wasn't thinking about anything else. I just ripped out my jack cable and ran."
A burnt guitar and effects pedal board were lying on the pavement outside the bar on Monday, with flowers left on a partially melted electric keyboard.
A fifth member of Tosakan, a singer called Din, was missing until Monday evening.
"Din has been found. Please wait for an update on his condition. Hoping for his safety," the band said in a social media post.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New Malaysian passport wins praise at home and abroad
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The enhanced Malaysian passport has received positive feedback from the public and overseas immigration authorities since its launch earlier this month, says Immigration Department director-general Datuk Zakaria Shaaban.
He said the new passport had drawn praise for its attractive design, faster issuance process and enhanced security features.
"So far, the response from the public has been very positive. The issuance process is easy and fast, while the new design is more attractive.
"Immigration authorities abroad were also impressed with the new passport design," he told reporters today.
Zakaria said one feature that stood out was the passport's colour photograph, which had attracted attention from foreign counterparts and Malaysians sharing it on social media.
As of July 10, about 28,000 enhanced passports had been issued, he said.
Zakaria also advised Malaysians not to rush to renew their passports if their existing documents remained valid.
"If your current passport is still valid for travel, there is no need to renew it now. Wait until it has less than six months' validity before applying for a new one.
"The existing passport remains valid and can still be used for international travel," he said.
Introduced on July 1, the
enhanced passport
is available with five-year and 10-year validity periods, priced at RM200 and RM350 respectively.
It features 94 new security features aimed at strengthening protection against forgery, alteration and misuse.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Complexity remorse in the electrification transition - A strategic view
Article:
THERE is a specific kind of buyer's remorse that will start building quietly in Malaysia's car market and I think the building blocks are caused by misframing of the current technology.
It has nothing to do with whether people believe in electric vehicles but rather what happens a few years after they buy one.
New energy vehicles - cars running partly or fully on batteries rather than petrol alone - will likely cross 15 per cent of total industry volume in Malaysia by the end of 2026, although there is a likelihood this may happen slightly earlier.
That is the threshold industry watchers have been waiting for: the point at which early-majority adoption typically stops being linear and starts compounding - the so-called "inflection point."
By the numbers, Malaysia is no longer an early-adopter NEV market. It entering the growth phase. But look closer at what's inside that 15 per cent, and the picture splits.
Pure battery electric vehicles (BEVs) - no petrol tank at all - account for only around eight per cent of total volume. The rest of the surge is coming from PHEVs and hybrids, cars that carry both a petrol engine and a battery.
There's now a second force at work on top of that split. On 1 July 2026, a new tax regime came into effect imposing stricter CIF (cost, insurance, freight) and power-output thresholds on fully-built-up (CBU) imported EVs.
In practice, this will squeeze the supply of affordable imported BEVs right at the moment demand for them is building although manufacturers continue to over BEVS at the same price due to existing or stocks already on the way.
It will happen and let's call what's affecting the Malaysian BEV market as a Regulatory Compression Event.
Before we go deeper into what this will mean to the industry as a whole, let's examine why buyers are choosing to carry two powertrains
The instinct to read the PHEV surge as reluctance toward full electrification canoe very misleading.
Anecdotal evidence available at this early stage of the PHEV market in Malaysia points to something closer to the opposite: consumer intent for BEVs is there, but it's being suppressed on two fronts at once - charging anxiety on the demand side, and very soon, a genuine shortage of ffordable imported BEVs on the supply side.
A plug-in hybrid lets a buyer drive electric when it's convenient and fall back on petrol when it isn't - it looks like an obvious insurance policy against a public charging network that is still uneven.
A significant number of purchasers, and I know of a handful, who have decided to make the transition via PHEV because they live in strata dwellings and have no access to home charging.
PHEVs, on the surface of it gives them access to EV technology while not having to worry about charging every day, and you do have to charge a PEHV every day if you want the full benefit of the technology, that is to say the extreme fuel economy.
This is what I mean by Complexity Remorse: the slow-dawning realisation, a couple of years into ownership, many owners wants all the benefits of a simpler BEV and none of the technical complexity of a hybrid.
Obviously this will also depend heavily on how quickly the government begin focusing their attention on building charging infrastructure rather than just providing market access to OEMs and giving buyers choice.
Since current PHEV buyers are most T20 and the ownership cycles for this cohort typically run three to five years. That puts a large share of today's PHEV buyers back in showrooms between roughly 2029 and 2030, cross-shopping their next car.
By then, two things should have moved: public charging infrastructure should have matured, and - just as importantly under the new tax regime - local CKD BEV production should have scaled enough to fill the affordability gap left by the CBU restrictions.
Once both happen, a significant share of this cohort should convert straight to full BEVs, in what amounts less to gradual adoption than to a rapid BEV correction - the market snapping back toward what underlying consumer intent already wanted.
Seen this way, today's PHEV sales are actually an incubation pool - a holding pattern of drivers already comfortable with electric motoring, waiting on infrastructure and supply rather than persuasion, giving way to BEV dominance by 2029/2030.
For OEMs, the strategic error would be treating a PHEV sale as a long-term shift in consumer preference and over-investing in dual-powertrain platforms as a result.
I would suggest that the more defensible play is designing the PHEV purchase explicitly as a stepping stone - using it as a "nursery" to build customer loyalty - while aligning CKD BEV production roadmaps to be robust enough to offer a seamless upgrade path by the 2029 replacement cycle.
As battery technology costs continue to fall - including newer chemistries like sodium-ion - OEMs should treat the BEV pipeline as the default upgrade path for this cohort, and weigh any further dual-powertrain investment against the high probability of a rapid market correction once
affordable CKD BEVs reach scale.
For policymakers, the current tax regime favours localisation, and that's a defensible industrial strategy - but it may result in brand owners over prioritising PHEVs and hybrid in the medium term due to the investment nature of local assembly operations that require at least a five year horizon.
This misapplication of capital can be very costly and dangerous for OEMs in Malaysia.
The market has already answered the question of whether Malaysians will adopt electrified vehicles once cost parity is reached - they will.
The government now needs to make sure the charging network is ready for the moment CKD BEV production ramps up to meet that demand.
The clearest lever available is mandating EV-ready strataguidelines: shared, interoperable AC destination charging built into new and existing high-rise developments.
Without it, the risk isn't just delay - it's a "lost generation" of electrified vehicle owners stuck in hybrids they can never fully charge, even after affordable BEVs finally arrive.
The demand for full electrification is not in question. What's in question is whether the infrastructure and the industry will be ready to meet it at the exact moment Complexity Remorse pushes today's hybrid buyers to make the switch.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Male infertility 'no longer overlooked' under BuAI programme
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government, through the National Population and Family Development Board (LPPKN), is providing special subsidies for B40 and M40 couples struggling to conceive.
The assistance is provided under the Fertility Treatment Assistance and Infertility Advocacy (BuAI) programme, said LPPKN chairman Datuk Seri Rohani Abdul Karim.
Speaking to reporters after the National-Level World Population Day 2026 celebration, Rohani said fertility treatment could be costly, and the subsidy was aimed at easing the financial burden on lower- and middle-income families.
"On fertility, that's back to us. We came out with a programme called BuAI. Many couples have been married but find it hard to conceive. Treatment is expensive.
"So the government, through the National Population and Family Development Board, gives special subsidies for B40 and M40 couples," she said.
Rohani said LPPKN had also expanded its approach to address male infertility, which had traditionally been overlooked, with difficulties in conceiving often assumed to be the wife's issue alone.
"In the past, people always blamed the wife if they couldn't have kids, but now we find it's also the men," she said.
"We have a couple who didn't give up after 12 years, and now she is seven months pregnant. We've even had twins and triplets from this programme," she added.
The New Straits Times
reported in May
that 47,000 couples were expected to benefit from the BuAI programme this year, with financing for up to two intrauterine insemination (IUI) cycles, prioritising the B40 group after screening.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pas open to cooperation, but focus remains on PN for Negri polls
Article:
KOTA BARU: Pas remains open to political cooperation but is focused on strengthening Perikatan Nasional (PN) ahead of the Negri Sembilan election.
Pas deputy president Datuk Seri Tuan Ibrahim Tuan Man said the party had always been open to discussions, but any cooperation would depend on both sides reaching a mutually beneficial outcome.
"Pas has consistently opened the door for discussions with any party that supports the principles of justice and rejects extremist groups.
"Negotiations are always possible, but they depend on the readiness of both parties to achieve a win-win outcome," he said.
For now, he said, Pas was concentrating on consolidating PN's machinery and preparations for the Negri Sembilan polls.
Asked about the coalition's preparations, Tuan Ibrahim said an official statement would be issued soon.
"Insya-Allah, there will be a statement in the near future," he said.
Yesterday, Umno president Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said the party had no agreement with Pas on
seat negotiations
for the Negri Sembilan election.
Responding to Pas president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang's claim that talks were ongoing, Zahid said any engagement was merely exploratory and had yet to result in a formal agreement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zeekr expands dealer network with three new partners
Article:
ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Malaysia (Zeekr Malaysia) is expanding its authorised dealer network as it seeks to strengthen its retail and aftersales presence nationwide amid growing demand for premium electric vehicles.
The company has appointed three new dealer partners - Quantum Motors Sdn Bhd, Wheelcorp Premium Sdn Bhd and Ghee Hua Co Sdn Bhd - to broaden its footprint in Peninsular Malaysia, Sabah and Sarawak.
Quantum Motors will operate outlets at IOI City Mall, Seremban and Kuala Lumpur North, while Wheelcorp Premium will cover Setia Alam and Ghee Hua will serve Kuching.
The latest appointments will increase Zeekr Malaysia's retail presence to 11 outlets nationwide.
The company plans to expand the network to 18 outlets by the end of 2026.
Some of the new facilities will operate as 3S centres, providing vehicle sales, service and spare parts to improve customer accessibility and aftersales support.
The dealer expansion comes as the brand continues to grow its product portfolio in Malaysia, following the introduction of the 2026 Zeekr X alongside the Zeekr 7X and Zeekr 009.
At the Kuala Lumpur International Mobility Show 2026, the company also opened bookings for the Zeekr 9X and Zeekr 009 Grand, marking its entry into the ultra-luxury EV segment.
General manager Eddy Lu said the expanded dealer network is aimed at improving the ownership experience by offering better access to sales and aftersales services.
He said Zeekr will continue working with its dealer partners to strengthen customer support and expand its nationwide ecosystem in line with future product launches and the company's long-term growth plans in Malaysia.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Invest Selangor launches 'BLAST' to integrate SMEs into global supply chain
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Invest Selangor Bhd has launched the Invest Selangor Business Linkages & Advancement Programme (BLAST), to assist small and medium enterprises (SMEs) integrate into high value global supply chains.
The programme, launched by Selangor executive councillor for investment, trade and mobility Ng Sze Han, seeks to connect local SMEs with multinational corporations (MNCs), mid tier companies (MTCs) and industry anchor companies to improve their competitiveness and market readiness.
BLAST is part of the Second Selangor Plan (RS-2) under the Local Market Integration Programme and builds on the state's industrial development efforts under the First Selangor Plan.
Through the initiative, participating SMEs will undergo a structured six month programme covering capability development, corporate mentorship and market readiness.
The programme will conclude with business matching sessions at the Selangor International Business Summit (SIBS) 2026, to be held from Oct 14 to 17 at the Kuala Lumpur Convention Centre (KLCC).
Unlike conventional one off business matching initiatives, BLAST is designed to foster long term partnerships between SMEs and larger industry players, enabling local companies to upgrade operational capabilities, meet international standards and expand into regional and global markets.
The inaugural intake comprises 12 SMEs across four strategic sectors identified as key growth areas for Selangor: aerospace, rail, life sciences and digital technology, with three companies selected from each sector.
Invest Selangor said the initiative underscores the state government's commitment to ensuring that investment inflows translate into sustainable opportunities for local businesses.
Selangor remained Malaysia's largest investment destination, securing RM33.5 billion in approved investments in the first quarter of 2026.
The state also recorded a gross domestic product of RM460.1 billion in 2025, accounting for 26.5 per cent of the national economy, the highest share among all states.
Speaking at the launch, Ng said that while Selangor took pride in its continued success in attracting investments, it was only part of the state's mission.
"Our greater responsibility is to ensure these investments generate meaningful and lasting opportunities for Selangor businesses, particularly our SMEs.
"BLAST is not simply about matching SMEs with strategic corporate partners. It is about preparing them for success.
"By strengthening their capabilities and connecting them to high value supply chains, we are empowering our SMEs to become globally competitive while maximising the long term benefits of investment for Selangor," he said in a statement.
The programme is being implemented in collaboration with the Malaysian Technology Development Corporation (MTDC), which will oversee the management and execution of the SME capacity building programme alongside corporate and ecosystem partners.
Invest Selangor said the collaboration would leverage the partners' expertise in mentorship, knowledge transfer and institutional networking to strengthen Selangor's industrial ecosystem and enhance the competitiveness of local enterprises.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New era for Gibraltar as border controls with Spain set to end
Article:
MADRID: Thousands of workers crossing daily between Spain and Gibraltar will enter a new era of easier travel on Wednesday, as border checks that have long been a source of tension are lifted.
Home to only around 40,000 people, the tiny self-governing British territory at the southern tip of the Iberian Peninsula relies on about 15,500 daily cross-border workers from Spain, who make up nearly half of its workforce.
During rush hours, long lines can form at the land border and documents are checked – especially during periods of tension between Britain and Spain, which claims sovereignty over Gibraltar.
But under an agreement reached between Brussels and London following Britain's exit from the European Union, border controls between Gibraltar and Spain will be eliminated from July 15.
A smoother border will make it easier for Gibraltar businesses to recruit and retain workers who live in Spain, as the "hassle" of crossing the frontier can be "significant", said Owen Smith, head of the Gibraltar Federation of Small Businesses.
"It's been a big factor in retention, and certainly a fluid border is going to make life much easier," he told AFP, calling it "very, very positive."
The agreement, which will be signed on Tuesday in Brussels, will align Gibraltar with the rules of Europe's passport-free Schengen travel area.
It was reached after years of talks between Spain, Britain and the EU.
Travellers arriving from outside the Schengen zone will still have to show their passports to officials at Gibraltar's airport and port.
Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez is set to visit the frontier zone on Wednesday, where workers have in recent weeks taken down the old chain-link fencing between Gibraltar and Spain.
He has hailed the new arrangements as bringing down "the last wall" inside the EU, saying they would create a zone of shared prosperity.
Gibraltar Chief Minister Fabian Picardo has described the agreement as removing "the physical barriers of a bygone era of friction" while keeping "the keys to our own front door."
The border was closed by Spanish dictator Francisco Franco in 1969 after Gibraltar, which relies on London for defence and foreign policy, voted overwhelmingly in a referendum to remain British.
The closure, which lasted 13 years, cut off the daily movement of workers from Spain into Gibraltar and separated families.
Since then, long queues have repeatedly formed at the Gibraltar-Spain border when diplomatic tensions over the territory's sovereignty have led to tighter controls by Spain.
"It is important that this sword of Damocles disappears," said Manuel Triano Paulete, secretary general of the CCOO trade union in Spain's Campo de Gibraltar region which surrounds the British territory, saying cross-border workers often did not know how long it would take them to get to work.
With an economy based on financial services and online gaming, Gibraltar – which covers just under seven square kilometres - has one of the highest per capita incomes in the world.
It has long been a lifeline for people who live in Campo de Gibraltar, which has historically had one of Spain's highest jobless rates.
London and Madrid have disputed control of Gibraltar since the tiny territory was ceded to Britain in the 1713 Treaty of Utrecht.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Boy's false kidnap report leads to school bullying case
Article:
ALOR SETAR: A 14-year-old schoolboy's fabricated report that he had been kidnapped in Padang Terap led police to uncover a separate bullying case he had allegedly endured at his former school.
Kedah police chief Datuk Adzli Abu Shah said the Padang Terap police headquarters initially received a report that the Form Two student had been abducted by two unidentified men in Kuala Nerang on Sunday.
Police later found the teenager by the roadside near Kota Putra, Durian Burung, in Padang Terap.
"In his initial statement, the boy claimed two suspects blocked his path, covered his eyes and mouth, and forced him into a vehicle.
"He also claimed his hands and feet were tied before he was abandoned by the roadside," he told a press conference, adding that public tip-offs later led police to the boy.
Adzli said police later discovered that the kidnapping claim was false.
He said the student had made up the story out of distress and embarrassment after mistakenly entering a girls' toilet at his school before the incident.
"The student said that after school, he mistakenly walked into a girls' toilet. As he was leaving, he encountered a female student who questioned his actions.
"The boy then fled the school compound but was pursued by the female student, who shouted at him, causing him severe distress."
The boy then hitched a ride on a motorcycle and got off at Kota Putra, Durian Burung.
Adzli said the boy pointed to a house and claimed it was his home, but the homeowner grew suspicious of his behaviour and contacted police.
He confirmed that the student was safe.
During questioning, however, the student revealed that he had been bullied at his previous school in Alor Setar before transferring to the new school in Kuala Nerang.
Records showed that the student had lodged a police report on June 18, a week after starting at the school in Kuala Nerang.
"He claimed he was forced to fetch food for senior students. Following the revelation, police arrested four students, aged between 16 and 17, from the Alor Setar school yesterday," Adzli said.
All four suspects were released on bail after their statements were recorded.
The false report is being investigated under Section 182 of the Penal Code for providing false information.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Six Pakatan lawmaker demand major rewrite of FOI Bill
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Six Pakatan Harapan lawmakers have called for major changes to the Freedom of Information Bill 2026, describing the government's draft as unsatisfactory and inferior to existing laws in Selangor and Penang.
Subang member of parliament Wong Chen said while they supported a federal freedom of information law, the Bill in its present form fell short of promoting transparency, accountability and openness.
"The current draft is unsatisfactory and falls short of the spirit and purpose of having freedom of information legislation," he said in a press conference at the Dewan Rakyat here today.
The other MPs were S. Kesavan (Sungai Siput-PH), Lee Chean Chung (Petaling Jaya-PH), Hassan Abdul Karim (Pasir Gudang-PH), Zahir Hassan (Wangsa Maju-PH) and Rodziah Ismail (Ampang-PH).
Wong said the Selangor and Penang Freedom of Information enactments, although imperfect, were substantially better and should be used as templates in redrafting the Bill.
He also urged the government to complete an advanced draft of the Ombudsman Bill and present it together with the FOI Bill to the new Parliamentary Special Select Committee.
"The FOI Bill is intended to work together with an Ombudsman Bill, which is referred to throughout the legislation. Yet MPs have not been presented with this Bill," he said.
Yesterday, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said announced that
the Bill
would be referred to a Parliamentary Special Select Committee for further scrutiny.
Civil society groups
welcomed the move
as they warned the Bill's proposed criminal penalties could intimidate journalists, deter whistleblowers and undermine investigative reporting.
Wong said the six MPs welcomed the government's decision to refer the FOI Bill to the select committee for further scrutiny, but requested that at least one of them be appointed to ensure their concerns and those raised by civil society groups were directly represented.
The concerns included the continued dominance of secrecy laws such as the Official Secrets Act 1972, broad disclosure exemptions, the absence of a meaningful public-interest test, restrictions on applicants and information requests, and the lack of an independent appeals mechanism.
Wong also criticised the legislative process, saying the Bill was only placed on their tables at 10am yesterday and that consultations without the actual legal text offered little scope for meaningful feedback.
He said MPs and stakeholders must receive draft legislation at least two weeks before tabling so concerns could be identified and addressed earlier.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UNHCR cardholders not above Malaysian law, says Immigration DG
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) cardholders are not exempt from enforcement action if they commit offences under Malaysian law, despite being allowed to remain in the country on humanitarian grounds, says Immigration Department director-general Datuk Zakaria Shaaban.
He said UNHCR cardholders were exempt only from immigration action for overstaying and not possessing valid immigration documents.
"We cannot take action against them for overstaying or not having valid documents if they hold a UNHCR card.
"However, they can still face action for other offences under the Immigration Act and other laws," he told reporters today.
He said undocumented foreigners without identification would be detained regardless of nationality.
"We do not care who they are. If they have no documents, they will be detained.
"We can also take action if they are working. The exemption applies only to overstaying and the lack of valid documents," he said.
Although Malaysia does not officially recognise UNHCR cards, Zakaria said the government accepted them as temporary documents on humanitarian grounds.
"It is only because of humanitarian considerations that we allow them to remain temporarily," he said.
On the number of Rohingya refugees in Malaysia, Zakaria said the department relied on UNHCR registration data as it did not have definitive figures.
He said the government was implementing a refugee registration programme to establish more accurate data, adding that many Rohingya were believed to be unregistered.
On June 11, the
Home Ministry
introduced the Refugee Registration and Status Determination programme for UNHCR cardholders and others claiming refugee status. The initiative is being spearheaded by the Immigration Department.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Khaled: Malaysia will not rely on single power bloc for defence needs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia will diversify its list of defence suppliers and avoid relying on a single power bloc to protect the Armed Forces from potential supply disruptions amid growing geopolitical uncertainty.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin said the country's procurement strategy was guided by national security interests and Malaysia's neutral and non-aligned foreign policy.
"The Defence Ministry consistently ensures that the country's defence procurement strategy is based on national security interests and does not depend on a single power bloc or supplier.
"This approach is in line with Malaysia's neutral and non-aligned foreign policy, while taking into account an increasingly challenging global geopolitical environment," he said in a written parliamentary reply to Datuk Seri Ismail Sabri Yaakob (Bera - BN).
He said the government would also prioritise government-to-government procurement arrangements to provide stronger supply chain guarantees, improve accountability and reduce the risk of disruptions or unilateral contract cancellations.
Khaled said the government would also strengthen the National Defence Industry Policy by promoting technology transfer, developing the domestic defence industry and expanding local maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO) capabilities.
He said these measures would reduce Malaysia's dependence on foreign suppliers and strengthen the country's defence self-reliance.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's falling fertility rate mirrors 'global trend'
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's Total Fertility Rate of 1.6, against a replacement rate of 2.1, reflects a global trend rather than a uniquely Malaysian crisis.
United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) assistant representative Tengku Aira Tengku Razif said the phenomenon is observed globally, with South Korea's rate being significantly lower at 0.9.
She also dismissed the notion that young people are avoiding marriage or children by choice, citing a global UNFPA survey.
"We used to think young people didn't want marriage or kids, but the survey proved otherwise," she told reporters at the National World Population Day 2026 celebration.
Tengku Aira said young people still desire families, but face systemic barriers such as a high cost of living, childcare challenges, and the struggle to balance employment.
She added that describing Malaysia's situation as "alarming" overlooks the global trend of the younger generation delaying marriage and childbearing for economic reasons.
According to the UNFPA survey, 88 per cent of respondents cited financial security and job stability as the primary factors shaping their family decisions.
Tengku Aira said addressing the issue requires cross-sectoral policies that extend beyond the mandate of a single agency.
"It is about the income levels required for people to feel comfortable and safe to have children," she said.
While UNFPA has not yet completed its full analysis for Malaysia, the agency intends to expand its Demographic Futures Survey locally to gain deeper insights, she said.
It was previously reported that Malaysia's Total
Fertility Rate
had fallen below the replacement level, declining from 2.1 children per woman in 2010 to 1.6 in 2022.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US lawmakers demand Pentagon release findings from probe of Iran school strike
Article:
WASHINGTON: Democratic senators led by Kirsten Gillibrand called on Monday for President Donald Trump's administration to disclose within the next week the findings from a US military investigation into a Feb 28 strike at a girls' school in Iran.
Reuters first reported on March 5 that an initial, internal US military investigation showed US forces were likely responsible for the fatal strike in Minab on the opening day of the war with Iran.
The group of more than two dozen US senators, including the top Democrat on the Senate Armed Services Committee, Jack Reed, requested in a letter the US military finalise its investigation, brief Congress and present a plan to ensure such a mistake does not happen again.
"There is no justification for withholding an unclassified accounting of what happened, what went wrong, and what the Department is doing to prevent recurrence," their letter said.
Asked for comment, a Pentagon official told Reuters: "The investigation is ongoing. We do not have any updates to announce at this time."
The strike killed more than 175 children and teachers, Iranian officials say. The lawmakers' letter notes that would make it the US military's largest civilian casualty incident since 1991, when it mistakenly bombed a shelter in Iraq, killing more than 400 civilians.
Archived copies of the Iranian school's official website show the school is adjacent ‌to a ⁠compound operated by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), the military force that reports to Iran's supreme leader.
Reuters, citing sources familiar with the matter, has reported that US officials responsible for creating targeting packages appeared to have used out-of-date intelligence.
US Admiral Brad Cooper, head of ​Central Command, which is directing the war effort, testified in May that the investigation was "complex" given ‌that the school was located on an active Iranian cruise missile base.
Trump, however, has cast doubt on whether the US military will ever know what happened given the amount of military activity at the start of the war.
"Somebody said it was our missile, maybe ​it wasn't our missile but I have seen nothing to lead me to believe ‌it ⁠was." Trump remarked on June 24, adding: "I don't think it was us."
Iranian officials have pointed to the strike on the school as a US war crime.
In the letter, the lawmakers ask Cooper and US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth to submit an unclassified version of the findings to Congress and the US public.
They also ask for a prevention and remediation plan "that identifies the specific corrective actions the Department will take to ensure this does not happen again."
"The United States military has a legal and moral obligation to take all feasible precautions to prevent civilian harm," the letter said.
"When a US strike kills civilians, the Department owes Congress, the American people, and the victims' families a clear accounting of what happened and a credible plan to prevent future failures."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's biggest income group can afford RM200,000-RM300,000 homes
Article:
PETALING JAYA: Malaysia's largest household income group earns between RM3,000 and RM5,000 a month, pointing to an affordable housing price range of about RM200,000 to RM300,000, according to the latest research by Rehda Institute.
The findings highlight a growing mismatch between household income trends and existing housing policies, with the research also showing that the number of households earning RM10,000 and above monthly has tripled since 2014.
Chairman Datuk Jeffrey Ng Tiong Lip said the changing income landscape has placed greater pressure on middle-income households, with M40 homeownership rates now falling below those of the B40.
"Today, the B40 leads the M40 in homeownership. Due recognition needs to be given to existing policies that year in and year out have not created the change that we want," he said at the pre-launch of the Regional Housing Conference 2026 today.
Ng said cross-subsidisation, where open market homes help fund price-controlled units, has pushed up prices for regular buyers, especially the middle-income group.
"Policies have not kept up with changing demand, leading to many unsold units, including homes which we label as affordable," he said.
Ng said rising prices have made homeownership increasingly difficult for M40 households, contributing to the decline in ownership within the segment.
"Housing for all means fair access. We need to make everyone be on an equal playing field."
Meanwhile, the Rehda Institute called for stronger collaboration between the government, researchers and industry players to improve policy formulation through collective analysis of housing data.
Ng said social and public housing should remain government-led, while supporting the establishment of a single national housing agency to better coordinate housing policies.
"In terms, if we want Malaysians of all income levels to keep owning homes, our housing policies must follow real needs and real data, not just old rules.
"Past policies did help many people own homes, but Malaysia has changed; our population, incomes and lifestyles are different now."
Meanwhile, the Rehda Institute has urged the government to adopt a data-driven framework for housing reforms to build a more balanced and inclusive housing market.
Chairman Datuk Jeffrey Ng Tiong Lip said housing policies should be guided by integrated data and analytics instead of fragmented information held across multiple agencies.
"We are recommending to the government that policies be based on data. Data is very important. It exists in the market, but it's not integrated," he said at the pre-launch of the Regional Housing Conference 2026 today.
Malaysia has abundant housing-related data across agencies, including the Housing Integrated Management System, National Property Information Centre, Department of Statistics Malaysia and Central Database Hub.
However, Ng said the information remains fragmented, limiting its effectiveness in supporting policy formulation and long-term housing planning.
"For the information to be utilised well for decision-making, these datasets must be integrated so that income, housing supply, demographics, transport and planning information can be formulated together instead of in isolation.
"AI and digital twins are powerful tools, but they only work with integrated, reliable information.
"These technologies can reveal mismatches before thousands of homes are built, telling us how many homes are needed, what types, where they should go, and how they connect to jobs and services," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Housing affordability must go beyond purchase price, says Syed Saddiq
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government should expand its definition of housing affordability beyond purchase price alone to include renovation, maintenance and other costs faced by young homebuyers, says Muar MP Syed Saddiq Syed Abdul Rahman.
Speaking during the Ministers' Question Time session in the Dewan Rakyat, Syed Saddiq said the current measure was largely based on income levels, with homes priced below RM300,000 generally considered affordable.
However, he said this did not reflect the full financial burden faced by buyers after securing a housing loan.
"Even if an affordable home is available and vacant, there are renovation costs, maintenance costs and additional expenses that need to be taken into account," he said in a supplementary question to Deputy Housing and Local Government Minister Datuk Aiman Athirah Sabu.
He added that many young buyers often had to fork out tens of thousands of ringgit after obtaining financing for homes priced around RM200,000.
Aiman said the ministry recognised various methods of measuring affordability, including the median multiple approach, where a home's price should be around three times the median household's annual gross income.
"The government's view is that housing affordability should not be measured based on a single national price alone," she said.
She said the government was also expanding access to financing, including through the MyHome guarantee housing loan scheme, which allows borrowing of up to 120 per cent of a property's value.
Under the scheme, a buyer purchasing a RM100,000 home could borrow up to RM120,000, with the additional 20 per cent covering renovation and related costs.
Aiman said efforts to help young Malaysians own their first homes required a whole-of-government approach involving federal and state governments as well as private developers.
"We continue to examine ways to ensure young people can own their first home while sustaining their family livelihoods," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man pleads not guilty to possessing firearms and 6,158 bullets
Article:
LUMUT: An unemployed man pleaded not guilty at the Seri Manjung Sessions Court here to three charges of possessing a rifle, five air rifles, and 6,158 bullets.
Ling Boon Xu, 30, made the plea after the charges were read out before judge Azizah Ahmad.
On the first charge, Ling was accused of possessing a 9mm calibre rifle in a wooden hut behind a house in Kampung Cina, Sitiawan, on the night of July 1.
The charge was framed under Section 8 of the Firearms (Increased Penalties) Act, which carries a maximum jail term of 14 years and not less than six strokes of the rotan upon conviction.
For the second charge, the unrepresented accused was charged with possessing five unlicensed air rifles.
The firearms comprised a Neo Rambo, a The Exclusive Model Crosman calibre 5.5mm, an X-1, an Airforce Automatic calibre 4.5mm/.177, and an Avenger calibre .25 (6.35mm) pellet rifle.
On the third charge, he was accused of possessing 6,158 rounds of ammunition without a valid licence.
Both the second and third charges were framed under Section 8 of the Arms Act, which provides for a jail term of up to seven years, a fine of up to RM10,000, or both, upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Nor Syamilah Jalaluddin requested that bail be denied as the offences are non-bailable.
The court allowed the prosecution's application and fixed Aug 20 for mention to allow the accused to appoint a lawyer.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: ‘Give us the time under the mandate,’ Anwar says as pressure mounts for early GE16 after Johor polls
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 14 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today brushed aside calls for an early general election, urging rivals to allow his administration to complete its full term to safeguard economic growth and political stability.
He said voters wanted the government to focus on running the country rather than being drawn into continuous political campaigning.
“Give us the time under the mandate that we have until the election. Then we can fight it out. I have no problem with that,” Anwar said during the Prime Minister’s Question Time in the Dewan Rakyat.
He was responding to Hulu Terengganu MP Datuk Rosol Wahid (PN), who asked if a snap election should be considered amid regional geopolitical tensions and domestic political developments.
The current Parliament’s term can run until February 2028 unless dissolved earlier, but speculation over an early general election has grown following political strains within the unity government.
The speculation intensified after Barisan Nasional, a key partner in Anwar’s federal coalition, scored a major victory in the Johor state election, winning 48 of the 56 state seats while Pakatan Harapan secured eight.
The result has raised questions over the balance of power between the two former rivals, which joined forces after the 2022 general election to form the unity government.
However, Anwar said his administration should be allowed to focus on delivering its mandate instead of being distracted by general election speculation.

Title: New national housing policy to set affordable home prices using local income data
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 14 — The government plans to use local income and housing demand data to set affordable home prices under the National Housing Policy 2026-2035.
Deputy Housing and Local Government Minister Datuk Aiman Athirah Sabu said the ministry was finalising the policy as part of efforts to address rising prices and mismatches between housing supply and demand.
“However, the determination of affordable housing prices cannot be based solely on construction costs. Instead, it must consider the balance between the people’s ability to own homes and developers’ capacity to carry out housing developments,” she told the Dewan Rakyat today.
She was responding to Muar MP Syed Saddiq Syed Abdul Rahman, who asked about measures to curb rising house prices and prevent excessive housing development that did not match local demand.
Aiman Athirah said the ministry had mapped affordable housing prices using state- and district-level median household income data from the Household Income and Basic Amenities Survey 2024.
The mapping used the median multiple method to determine affordable price ranges based on residents’ income levels in specific locations.
She said the ministry was also using data from the Department of Statistics Malaysia, the National Property Information Centre, the Malaysian Urban Observatory and other agencies to determine the type, location and number of homes needed.
“This approach enables housing developments to be planned based on the actual needs of residents according to states and localities.
“It also helps the federal and state governments determine the type, location and number of homes required so that development is better aligned with market demand and household affordability,” she said.
Aiman Athirah said the National Affordable Housing Council, chaired by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, coordinated housing policies between the federal and state governments and addressed strategic issues affecting affordable housing provision.

Title: Housing Ministry says 1,615 troubled housing projects worth RM150.86b rehabilitated since 2022
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 14 — The government rehabilitated 1,615 delayed, sick and abandoned housing projects involving 190,422 units with a gross development value of RM150.86 billion between December 2022 and May 2026.
Deputy Housing and Local Government Minister Datuk Aiman Athirah Sabu said the achievement was part of the ministry’s efforts to address stalled private housing projects through a task force established in December 2022.
“The task force for delayed, sick and abandoned private housing projects was established in December 2022 to monitor these projects, and it is chaired by me.
“To date, 1,615 housing projects involving 190,422 housing units with a GDV of RM150.86 billion have been successfully rehabilitated between December 2022 and May 2026,” she told the Dewan Rakyat today.
She was responding to Muar MP Syed Saddiq Syed Abdul Rahman, who asked about measures to curb rising house prices and prevent excessive housing development that did not match local demand.
Aiman Athirah said the Ministry of Housing and Local Government acted as a mediator between homebuyers and developers to help revive problematic projects.
She said the ministry facilitated rescue efforts involving original developers, rescue developers or liquidators until projects obtained the Certificate of Fitness for Occupation or Certificate of Completion and Compliance.
The ministry also blacklisted developers and company directors involved in sick and abandoned projects.
“Developers and board members involved will be prevented from applying for new development licences, while their existing Housing Development Accounts (HDA) will also be frozen,” she added.
Aiman Athirah said the ministry also worked with state governments, local authorities, government agencies, utility providers and developers to identify problems and find solutions for affected projects.
It also conducted regular briefings to ensure developers understood their responsibilities under the Housing Development (Control and Licensing) Act 1966.
Other measures included encouraging the use of the Industrialised Building System and alternative construction materials to speed up completion and reduce costs without compromising quality.
